book_summary: Deuteronomy has chapters 1-11 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. Moses' opening discourse retells Horeb, distributed judicial leadership, the scouting failure, protected borders around Edom/Moab/Ammon, morally catastrophic wars against Sihon and Og, Torah as wisdom, Horeb's formless voice, the Decalogue, the Shema, wilderness provision, the warning against self-made wealth and national self-righteousness, renewed tablets, justice for orphan/widow/sojourner, ecological dependence on rain, and the blessing/curse choice. Safeguards reject colonial conquest, genocide, child killing, ethnic supremacy, forced endogamy, iconoclasm, antisemitism, supersessionism, prosperity theology, inherited-guilt abuse, disaster blame, and modern territorial weaponization.

chapters.notes: Horeb departure, distributed judges, Kadesh scouting crisis, wilderness judgment, Joshua succession, and failed unauthorized invasion.
Protected Edom/Moab/Ammon borders, paid food and water, wilderness provision, Sihon diplomacy/hardening, and total-destruction warfare under moral protest.
Og conquest, Rephaim lore, Transjordan allocation, reciprocal armed obligation, Joshua encouragement, and Moses' denied entry.
Torah wisdom, heard voice without form, anti-image warning, exile and compassionate return, Divine uniqueness, and east-Jordan refuge cities.
Present-generation Horeb covenant, Ten Words, Sabbath grounded in liberation, mediation, and command to walk without turning.
Shema, total covenant love, daily teaching, tefillin/mezuzah continuity, inherited abundance and forgetting, Massah, and Exodus catechesis.
Seven-nation herem commands, intermarriage restriction, sacred-site destruction, treasured-people election, covenant love, and fertility/health rhetoric under maximum safeguards.
Wilderness testing, manna, not-by-bread-alone, good-land abundance, gratitude, and warning against self-made wealth mythology.
Conquest is not proof of righteousness, stiff-necked internal critique, golden calf, broken tablets, and Moses rejecting replacement through intercession.
Second tablets, ark, Levite vocation, heart circumcision, impartial Divine justice, orphan/widow protection, and love of the sojourner.
Eyewitness memory, rain-dependent land, second Shema paragraph, ecological blessing/curse rhetoric, daily teaching, territorial promise, and Gerizim/Ebal.

translation_notes: Deuteronomy is Moses' retrospective covenant interpretation and must be compared with Exodus, Leviticus, and Numbers rather than flattened into a seamless transcript.
Distributed judges hear small and great, Israelite and sojourner, without partiality; foundational leadership does not mean solitary authority.
Protected borders and paid resources for Edom, Moab, and Ammon establish a restraint ethic that stands in unresolved tension with herem warfare against other peoples.
Total-destruction language naming men, women, and children is source-preserved and categorically rejected as modern ethics.
Horeb revelation is voice without form; anti-image texts must not authorize destruction of another community's art or sacred places.
The Decalogue begins with liberation from slavery, and Sabbath extends rest to workers, immigrants, children, and animals.
The Shema and its second paragraph are central living Jewish liturgy; tefillin and mezuzah practices must be honored without Christian appropriation or supersession.
Election and treasured-people language express Jewish covenant responsibility, not racial superiority or replacement by another religious community.
Not-by-bread-alone does not spiritualize hunger: the text first insists that the hungry were fed manna.
Wealth is not proof of righteousness; the warning targets the self-made myth that forgets liberation, dependence, labor, and gift.
Stiff-necked language is internal covenant critique and may never be used as an antisemitic stereotype.
Moses refuses replacement theology by interceding for the very people whose destruction could have benefited him.
Divine greatness in Deuteronomy 10 is defined through impartiality, refusal of bribery, justice for orphan and widow, and food, clothing, and love for the sojourner.
Rain and drought rhetoric requires ecological humility and must never become blame directed at victims of famine, climate disaster, or poverty.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Deuteronomy 1-3 retellings require systematic comparison with Numbers 13-14, 20-21, and 32, including differences in agency and chronology.
Deuteronomy 2-3 Sihon/Og herem rhetoric requires maximum anti-genocide and archaeological/historical-context audit.
Deuteronomy 4 anti-image and exile-return language requires Jewish reception, anti-iconoclasm, and anti-supersession safeguards.
Deuteronomy 5 Decalogue wording requires comparison with Exodus 20, especially Sabbath rationale and coveting order.
Deuteronomy 6 Shema wording, echad semantics, tefillin, mezuzah, and Jewish liturgical reception require careful source and reception audit.
Deuteronomy 7 election, herem, intermarriage, health, and fertility rhetoric require anti-supremacy, anti-miscegenation, disability, and infertility safeguards.
Deuteronomy 8 manna and discipline imagery require anti-prosperity, anti-abuse, and material-hunger safeguards.
Deuteronomy 9 golden-calf chronology and intercession require Exodus 32-34 comparison and explicit anti-replacement-theology framing.
Deuteronomy 10 itinerary differences, Levite inheritance, heart-circumcision metaphor, and sojourner love require source and Jewish-reception audit.
Deuteronomy 11 second-Shema paragraph, rain theology, Dathan/Abiram recall, and territorial limits require ecological, liturgical, and anti-colonial safeguards.
next_actions: Continue in another genuinely large batch with Deuteronomy 12-20: centralized worship, blood and food laws, false-prophet and apostasy tests, release and poverty protections, festivals, judges and kings, priestly/prophetic authority, refuge cities, witnesses, warfare exemptions, siege rules, and the prohibition on destroying fruit trees—with anti-violence, anti-persecution, anti-monarchy, economic-justice, and ecological safeguards.
